judgments.title: VIGILANT VENTURES SDN BHD v Lembaga Kemajuan Ikan Malaysia (LKIM)
judgments.catchwords: Contract Law – Formation and interpretation – Collaboration Agreement – Duration clause – Intention for 30 years subject to detailed extension mechanism – Trial period and automatic extensions – Further extensions require mutual written agreement – No unilateral right to compel extension after second automatic extension Contract Law – Expiry by effluxion of time – Distinguished from termination for breach – Agreement expiring by its own terms does not require notice of default or termination – Contract simply comes to an end Contract Law – Fraud and misrepresentation – Contract induced by fraud remains valid until rescinded – Finding of fraud relevant to damages, not to contractual expiry – Not contradictory to finding that contract was lawfully entered into Contract Law – Damages – Special damages – Must be strictly pleaded and strictly proved – Supported by documentary evidence (invoices, receipts, accounts) – Loss of guaranteed profits – Failure to prove actual gross income – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – General damages – Global award of RM2.4 million – No proper legal basis pleaded (account of profits/unjust enrichment not pleaded) – No strict proof of actual loss – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – Election between reliance loss and expectation loss – Claimant must elect – Cannot claim both Land Law – Vacant possession – Holding over after expiry of agreement – Registered proprietor entitled to vacant possession – Order affirmed

judgments.title: YS CHONG ENTERPRISE SDN BHD v PERKASA JAUHARI SDN BHD (IN LIQUIDATION)
judgments.catchwords: Construction Industry Payment and Adjudication Act 2012 – a question of considerable Doctrinal importance at the intersection of construction law and insolvency law: what is the effect, upon a winding-up order against a company which was founded upon an adjudication decision obtained by that company’s creditor, of a subsequent final determination by the court which conclusively reverses that adjudication decision on the merits - does the winding up order — by itself, and notwithstanding that the adjudication decision upon which it was founded has been extinguished under s. 13(c) of CIPAA by the final court determination — have the effect of transmuting the adjudication decision into a binding, permanent debt which survives that final determination, and which the CIPAA claimant may thereafter invoke as a mutual set-off under s. 526 of the Companies Act 2016 (“CA 2016”) against a winding-up petition subsequently presented by the aggrieved debtor company whose claims were vindicated in those very court proceedings? - the meaning of “finally decided by arbitration or the court” in s. 13(c), whether final determination means the decision must have been “decided, and no longer open to any form of challenge or appeal.

judgments.catchwords: Construction Industry Payment and Adjudication Act 2012 – a question of considerable Doctrinal importance at the intersection of construction law and insolvency law: what is the effect, upon a winding-up order against a company which was founded upon an adjudication decision obtained by that company’s creditor, of a subsequent final determination by the court which conclusively reverses that adjudication decision on the merits - does the winding up order — by itself, and notwithstanding that the adjudication decision upon which it was founded has been extinguished under s. 13(c) of CIPAA by the final court determination — have the effect of transmuting the adjudication decision into a binding, permanent debt which survives that final determination, and which the CIPAA claimant may thereafter invoke as a mutual set-off under s. 526 of the Companies Act 2016 (“CA 2016”) against a winding-up petition subsequently presented by the aggrieved debtor company whose claims were vindicated in those very court proceedings? - the meaning of “finally decided by arbitration or the court” in s. 13(c), whether final determination means the decision must have been “decided, and no longer open to any form of challenge or appeal.

judgments.title: HENG OOI HONG v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: STATUTORY INTERPRETATION Catchwords: Construction of statutes - Rules of statutory interpretation - Literal rule - Golden rule - Purposive approach - Interpretation Acts 1948 and 1967, Section 17A - Grammatical meaning - Conjunctive word "and" (dan) - Whether literal interpretation yields an absurd or conflicting outcome - Word "and" construed as disjunctive "or" (atau) - Separation of powers - Role of the judiciary - Strict limits on reading additional words into penal statutes - Legislative defect or lacuna - Remedy lying exclusively with the Legislature - Subsequent legislative amendment - Fisheries (Amendment) Act 2025 (Act A1780) - Effect of subsequent amendment as conclusive legislative acknowledgment of a lacuna. CRIMINAL PROCEDURE Catchwords: Revision - Revisionary jurisdiction of the High Court - Criminal Procedure Code, Section 323 - Courts of Judicature Act 1964, Section 31 - supervisory and revisionary power over criminal proceedings in subordinate courts - Scope of revision - Correction of illegal, incorrect, or improper orders - Distinction between revision and coordinate review - Plea of guilty - Voluntary and informed plea - Criminal Procedure Code, Section 173(b) - Strict pre-plea procedural safeguards - Failure of trial Judge to ensure accused fully understands nature and consequences of plea - Consequential livelihood-depriving penalties - Cancellation of commercial fishing license - Failure to state consequential penalties on the face of the charge sheet - Failure of prosecution to provide written notice - Failure of court to explain penalty prior to recording plea - Whether post-plea verbal assertions by prosecuting officer can cure a procedural defect - Forfeiture - Mandatory vs discretionary forfeiture - Forfeiture ordered under wrong statutory power - Attempt by prosecution to retrospectively substitute discretionary forfeiture under Section 34 of the Fisheries Act 1985 to cure defective mandatory forfeiture order under Section 52(1). FISHERIES Catchwords: Offences - Fisheries Act 1985, Section 8(a), Section 11(3)(a), Section 14(4)(b) - Operating vessel without valid license - Consequential sentencing orders - Forfeiture and disposal of vessel - Fisheries Act 1985, Section 52(1) - Prerequisites for mandatory vessel forfeiture - Necessity to satisfy both limb (a) and limb (b) of Section 52(1) conjunctively - Charge of operating vessel without valid license - Impossibility of violating license conditions for a non-existent license - Ultra vires order - Charge of using a license issued in the name of another person under Section 14(4)(b) - Omission of Section 14(4)(b) from the mandatory forfeiture regime of Section 52(1)(a) - Failure of subordinate court to bifurcate charges - Cancellation of fishing license - Fisheries Act 1985, Section 33(1) - Summary cancellation of license belonging to an uncharged third-party owner - Jurisdictional prerequisites of Section 33(1) - Nexus between license and physical offence. CONSTITUTIONAL LAW / NATURAL JUSTICE Catchwords: Fundamental liberties - Right to a fair procedure - Article 5(1) of the Federal Constitution - Rules of natural justice - Right to be heard (audi alteram partem) - Summary deprivation of proprietary right and primary livelihood - Cancellation of fishing license belonging to a registered third party - Third party never joined as a party to criminal proceedings or afforded opportunity to show cause - Unconstitutionality of condemning an unheard third party through the side-wind of an operator's guilty plea. WORDS & PHRASES Catchwords: "And" (dan) - "Or" (atau) - Fisheries Act 1985, Section 52(1) - Conjunctive vs disjunctive construction - "Owner" (pemunya) - Fisheries Act 1985, Section 2.

judgments.title: Pendakwa Raya v DEVABALAN A/L MARIAPPAN
judgments.catchwords: Criminal trial - the Accused pleaded guilty to the alternative charge in BA-45A-84-09/2025 and the charge in BA-45-39-09/2025 Mitigation - the Accused submits that a lenient sentence is appropriate and warranted in this matter, as he has pleaded guilty to all charges at the earliest available opportunity, no previous convictions, repentant, is now 31 years of age with strong prospects for rehabilitation and the weight of the illicit substances involved is not substantial - learned DPP, on the other hand, vehemently submits that an exemplary and deterrent sentence must be imposed upon the Accused - the public interest and deterrence are still the overriding consideration as well as the drugs involved are classified as highly potent and hardcore narcotics. Sentence - this Court is mindful that the Accused has pleaded guilty to all charges at the earliest available opportunity - this action not only demonstrates his immediate acceptance of responsibility but has saved valuable judicial time and public resources by avoiding a protracted trial - the plea of guilty of the Accused stands as a primary mitigating factor - the Accused also is a first-time offender which reflects that he is not a hardened or habitual criminal - nevertheless, the above mitigating factors do not absolve this Court from its duty to uphold the public interest and apply the necessary elements of both general and specific deterrence - a balanced approach is paramount - illicit drugs remain the prime national enemy, inflicting severe socio-economic harm upon society - a lenient sentence in this matter would fail to reflect the gravity of the offence and undermine the Government’s strict stance and unwavering policy in combatting this critical menace - the present case involves drugs which are classified as highly potent and hardcore narcotics - possess an exceptionally high risk of addiction and are notorious for causing catastrophic, irreversible physical and psychological destruction to users, thereby posing a severe threat to public health - the Accused’s criminal conduct extended far beyond drug possession - in attempting to evade arrest, the Accused exhibited a total disregard for public safety, causing extensive property damage to both police vehicle and multiple civilian vehicles - this reckless behaviour endangered innocent lives and significantly exacerbates the gravity of the offence.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Sri Shamsulbahri bin Haji Ibrahim

physical presence in or proximity to vehicle amounts to possession – Whether momentary presence in vehicle accessible to others sufficient to establish custody or control – Whether non-ownership of undermines proof of exclusive possession – Statutory presumptions – Presumption of trafficking – s. 37(da)(xvi) Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 – Presumption of possession – s. 37(d) Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 – Presumption as to specially constructed or adapted compartment – s. 37(h) Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 – Whether presumption of trafficking can arise absent affirmative proof of possession – Whether a fact may be established by presumption alone absent proved facts or inferences properly drawn therefrom – Whether double invocation of statutory presumptions is permissible – Whether a motorcar amounts to 'anything whatsoever' for purposes of presumption of custody – Whether existence of specially constructed compartment established absent expert or technical evidence EVIDENCE: Chain of custody of exhibits – Whether unexplained break in chain of custody at point of seizure, and unexplained delay before handover to investigating officer, casts doubt on integrity of exhibits subsequently analysed – Whether absence of contemporaneous marking or identification of exhibit by seizing officers renders provenance of exhibit unreliable – Opinion evidence of chemist – Whether chemist required to descend into granular detail of every step taken in laboratory – Whether analysis of entirety, as opposed to representative sample, of substance affects reliability of finding on net weight – Confessions and admissible information – s. 27 Evidence Act 1950 – s. 37B Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 – Whether non-verbal conduct, such as an unrecorded nod, capable of amounting to information or a statement – Whether discovery of incriminating article must be attributable exclusively to information given by accused – Whether police possession of prior knowledge of location of article precludes application of s. 27 – Forensic and DNA evidence – Whether absence of accused's DNA on principal control surfaces of vehicle undermines inference of exclusive custody and control – Whether presence of multiple unidentified third-party DNA profiles, uninvestigated by prosecution, undermines inference of accused's knowledge – Whether total absence of fingerprint evidence further undermines prosecution's case – Adverse inference – s. 114(g) Evidence Act 1950 – Whether failure to call material witness (identified transferee of vehicle; qualified technical or automotive expert) warrants adverse inference against prosecution – Whether failure to investigate or exclude alternative suspect connected with same operation warrants adverse inference – Inadequate invest

judgments.title: BLG SWIFT LOGISTIC SDN BHD v PANTRANS HAULAGE SDN BHD
judgments.catchwords: [1] Six (6) new BMW vehicles were damaged due to the alleged negligence of a trailer driver transporting the said vehicles from Kulim to Port Klang. At the time, an employee of the defendant ("Pantrans") was driving the trailer, which overturned, causing the BMW vehicles to be considered total losses. The dispute began between the plaintiff ("BLG"), a vehicle transportation logistics company and Pantrans, and now involves a third party, Tokio Marine, which Pantrans has brought into the proceedings. [2] Specifically, BMW Malaysia Sdn Bhd ("BMW Malaysia") claimed its losses under its own insurance policy, which was underwritten by Lonpac Insurance Berhad ("Lonpac"). This ends BMW Malaysia's claim against any other party. As a result of Lonpac's payment to BMW Malaysia, Lonpac filed an arbitration claim against BLG. In order to resolve this issue, BLG and Lonpac reached a final settlement. [3] As a consequence, BLG has asked Pantrans to pay it the same amount as BLG paid to Lonpac. BLG relies on two Transportation Agreements it entered into with Pantrans to make this claim. [4] In the meantime, Pantrans claims that the incident was not the result of its driver's negligence. It should be noted, however, that Pantrans has a claim against Tokio Marine on the basis of an indemnity pursuant to its Goods in Transit Insurance Policy that it obtained from Tokio Marine to cover such losses if they materialise. [5] In the case of Tokio Marine, it is defending Pantrans's claim against it with the argument that the policy is no longer enforceable due to the triggering of the non-contribution clause which results from BMW Malaysia's original losses being paid by Lonpac. Since there is a double insurance recovery process on the same subject matter, when Lonpac initiated its claim against BLG and BLG subsequently paid Lonpac, Tokio Marine is no longer liable towards Pantrans’s claim, especially when there are no excess amounts over and above the policy limit of the Goods in Transit Insurance Policy. [6] The Court finds that Pantrans is liable to BLG's claim. It cannot be denied that in the dispute before the Court, the incident occurred when Pantrans had the BMW vehicles in its possession, custody, or control. There is not a single element of fault on BLG's part for the occurrence of the incident. [7] As regards the third-party claim by Pantrans against Tokio Marine, the Court finds that given the purpose and effect of the Non Contribution Clause ("NCC"), it is clear and unambiguous that Tokio Marine has no obligation to indemnify Pantrans based on the circumstances before the Court. This implies that Tokio Marine is entitled to rely on the NCC's effect in creating an exclusion or escape clause. In this regard, by explicitly limiting its liability under the GIT Policy through the NCC only in regard to any excess amount beyond the amount payable by Lonpac, Tokio Marine can rely on the fact that since BLG had only paid RM1,750,000.00 by way of a settlement and not the full amount of RM2,298,943.39, Tokio Marine is not liable to pay any amount to indemnify BLG. In contrast, if BLG had paid RM2,298,943.39, Tokio Marine would be liable only for the excess amount over RM2,000,000.00, which is RM298,943.39. [8] With this in mind, the Court allows BLG's claim against Pantrans with costs of RM50,000.00, subject to allocatur. [9]The Court also dismisses Pantrans's claim against Tokio Marine with costs of RM35,000.00, subject to allocatur.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Raja Ahmad Mohzanuddin Shah bin Raja Mohzan

judgments.catchwords: Review jurisdiction - Rule 137 of the Rules of the Federal Court 1995 -Inherent powers of Federal Court – All sentences passed to be in accordance with the law – Avoiding miscarriage of justice - Exceptional circumstances - Fresh evidence of prisoner’s death following whipping. Constitutional Law - Federal Constitution - Articles 5(1), 8(1) and 8(2) Fundamental liberties - Life and personal liberty - Human dignity - Equal protection of the law - Whether it is in violation of medical ethics for medical officer to be participate in the execution of whipping. Judicial whipping - Corporal punishment - Whether judicial whipping amounts to cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment (“CIDT”) - Whether punishment of whipping is inconsistent with constitutional protection of life, dignity and bodily integrity. Whether whipping is disproportionate and therefore contrary to Article 8(1) - Whether exemption of women from whipping constitutes prohibited discrimination on ground only of gender is contrary to Art 8(2) - Intelligible differentia. Whether whipping is against Art. 5 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (“UDHR”) and the ASEAN Human Rights Declaration in its prohibitions against CIDT - Whether whipping is against customary international law.

judgments.title: KAMALESWARAN THAVER A/L SUPPIAH v Pendakwa Raya & ors
judgments.catchwords: Prosedur Jenayah — Rayuan terhadap sabitan dan hukuman — Tertuduh didakwa mengedar dadah berbahaya di bawah s 39B(1)(a) Akta Dadah Berbahaya 1952 (ADB) - boleh dihukum di bawah s 39B (2) ADB — Sama ada pembelaan dipertimbangkan secukupnya oleh Hakim Perbicaraan — Sama ada hakim perbicaraan terkhilaf dalam keputusan – Mahkamah ini mengurangkan pertuduhan kepada pertuduhan di bawah seksyen 12(2) ADB, dan boleh dihukum di bawah s 39A(2) ADB - Tertuduh dijatuhkan hukuman pemenjaraan selama 10 tahun dari tarikh tangkapan dan 10 kali sebatan rotan. Keterangan – dalam kes menyokong tuduhan pemilikan dadah dan bukannya pengedaran dadah.
